ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297672, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297679, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297899, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5005-4070-70db-b641-92a81342c9fe', conversation_id='019f5005-4070-70db-b641-92a7a6b770d6')

- `IsDeleted` -> 0

- `Id` -> from source `id`
- `Name` -> from source `name`, NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` -> from source `project_status__c`, enum: Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled. Map/normalize.
- `Go_Live_Date__c` -> from source `go_live_date__c`. Date parsing needed. Nullable in target.
- `Account__c` -> from source `account__c`. Map to generated Account.Id.
- `Opportunity__c` -> from source `opportunity__c`. This references an Opportunity. Should it map to Opportunity.Id? Target is text. Rule says cross-table keys must match. I'll note mapping strategy (direct copy if formats match, else transform).

- `IsDeleted` -> 0

### Installed_Asset__c (from fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c)
- `Id` -> from source `id`

- `Serial_Number__c` -> from source `serial_number__c`

**Cross-table key strategy:**
Source `accountid` (in Contact, Opportunity) and `account__c` (in Project__c, Installed_Asset__c) are text. The target `AccountId` and `Account__c` expect Salesforce-style Account `Id`. I need to define a key transform or join to map these source account keys to the generated/transformed Account.Id in the target. I will note that a `ACCOUNT_KEY_MAP` CTE or join is required to resolve raw source account keys to the target Account.Id.

**Enum Mapping Rules:**

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297672, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:

- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297679, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 12, 29, 297899, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f5005-4070-70db-b641-92a81342c9fe', conversation_id='019f5005-4070-70db-b641-92a7a6b770d6')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 25, 58, 298866, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Account`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 27, 49, 492113, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: Cross-Table Key Strategy & Global Conventions:
1. **Account ID Resolution**: Source tables (Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c) store raw account identifiers in `accountid` or `account__c`. Target columns (`AccountId`, `Account__c`) require the generated Salesforce-style Account.Id. A shared lookup/normalization strategy must be applied: strip prefixes/suffixes if formats differ, or join source keys against the transformed Source.Account to resolve correct Target.Account.Id.
2. **Opportunity ID Resolution**: Project__c references `opportunity__c`. Ensure this foreign key uses the exact same normalization logic as Opportunity.Id mapping to maintain referential integrity across models.
3. **Enum Normalization**: All enum fields must be case-insensitive matched against declared domains. Apply `TRIM(LOWER(...))` for comparison, then map to exact target casing (e.g., INITCAP). Unmapped or missing values default to NULL (except where NOT NULL constraints force a fallback like '1900-01-01' for dates).
4. **Date Parsing**: Source dates use mixed formats (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, etc.). Always parse via conditional `TO_DATE` or regex extraction with fallbacks; never cast raw strings to date types blindly. Output standardized ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD'.
5. **Amount Cleaning**: Strip currency symbols/prefixes. Handle European decimal formatting (remove thousand-separator dots if a comma exists later in the string, then replace comma with period). Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION. NULL for non-numeric after cleaning.
6. **Audit Columns**: Source lacks CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate/IsDeleted. Default to '2024-01-01' or NULL for date fields, and `0` for IsDeleted across all models unless business rules specify dynamic generation.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 31, 39, 951770, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

# Transformation guidelines

- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model

- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='I see, `query_postgres` is not available in this environment. I'll proceed with the transformation based on the provided schema and guidelines. Let me construct the dbt model for Opportunity.

From the guidelines:
- Source table: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity`
- Columns: id, name, stagename, closedate, amount, currencyisocode, accountid
- Target columns mapping as specified in the approved mapping plan

For AccountId resolution, since I don't have access to the source Account data to do a join/lookup, I'll need to handle it carefully. The guidelines say "Resolve to target Account.Id using shared normalization/lookup". Since Opportunity and Account come from the same source schema, but I'm only producing one model for Opportunity here, I'll assume that the `accountid` in the opportunity table already matches or can be directly mapped to the Account.Id (which likely comes from the source account's id). I'll just pass it through as is, possibly with TRIM.

Wait, the guidelines say "Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns". Since I can't run queries, I'll assume `accountid` in opportunity matches `id` in account directly after trimming whitespace.

Let's construct the SQL:

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT 
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(INITCAP(name)), 'Unnamed Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'prospecting' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'qualification' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'proposal/price quote' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'closed won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(stagename)) = 'closed lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- fallback for NOT NULL constraint
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE 
            WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
            WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN SUBSTR(closedate, 1, 4) || '-' || SUBSTR(closedate, 5, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(closedate, 7, 2)
            WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
            ELSE NULL 
        END, 
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(amount) != '' THEN
            CAST(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'), -- strip non-numeric except dots and commas
                    '^(\d+)(\.\d{3})+(,\d+)$', -- European format with thousand separators (dots) and decimal (comma)
                    '\1\2' -- remove dots
                )::TEXT,
                'DOUBLE PRECISION'
            ) * 10 -- wait, need to handle comma as decimal properly
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "Amount",
```

Wait, let's be more careful with the European number parsing. The pattern is:
- If it contains both `.` and `,` where `,` is the last non-digit, assume European format (dots are thousands, comma is decimal)
- If only commas or only dots, need to decide. Standard US is dot for decimal, no thousand separators.

A simpler approach often used:
```sql
CASE 
    WHEN amount IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(amount) != '' THEN
        CASE 
            -- European format: contains comma after removing currency symbols and spaces
            WHEN amount ~ ',' THEN 
                REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,]', '', ''), '\.', '')::DOUBLE PRECISION / 1000.0 -- wait this is wrong if it's also a decimal
            ELSE

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 43, 47, 673922, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text

- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: Cross-Table Key Strategy & Global Conventions:
1. **Account ID Resolution**: Source tables (Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c) store raw account identifiers in `accountid` or `account__c`. Target columns (`AccountId`, `Account__c`) require the generated Salesforce-style Account.Id. A shared lookup/normalization strategy must be applied: strip prefixes/suffixes if formats differ, or join source keys against the transformed Source.Account to resolve correct Target.Account.Id.
2. **Opportunity ID Resolution**: Project__c references `opportunity__c`. Ensure this foreign key uses the exact same normalization logic as Opportunity.Id mapping to maintain referential integrity across models.
3. **Enum Normalization**: All enum fields must be case-insensitive matched against declared domains. Apply `TRIM(LOWER(...))` for comparison, then map to exact target casing (e.g., INITCAP). Unmapped or missing values default to NULL (except where NOT NULL constraints force a fallback like '1900-01-01' for dates).
4. **Date Parsing**: Source dates use mixed formats (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, etc.). Always parse via conditional `TO_DATE` or regex extraction with fallbacks; never cast raw strings to date types blindly. Output standardized ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD'.
5. **Amount Cleaning**: Strip currency symbols/prefixes. Handle European decimal formatting (remove thousand-separator dots if a comma exists later in the string, then replace comma with period). Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION. NULL for non-numeric after cleaning.
6. **Audit Columns**: Source lacks CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate/IsDeleted. Default to '2024-01-01' or NULL for date fields, and `0` for IsDeleted across all models unless business rules specify dynamic generation.
Source tables: fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c
Join keys: account__c, opportunity__c
- Id <- id
- Name <- name (TRIM(INITCAP(...)); COALESCE to 'Unnamed Project' if NULL)
- Project_Status__c <- project_status__c (TRIM(LOWER(...)); map to exact INITCAP enum (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled); fallback NULL)

- Account__c <- account__c (Resolve to target Account.Id using shared normalization/lookup. Format mismatch likely between source and generated keys.)
- Opportunity__c <- opportunity__c (Direct copy or join to Opportunity.Id if format differs. Ensure consistent key transform used everywhere Project references Opportunity.)
- Legacy_Project_ID__c <- id (Direct copy from source PK)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Static default or NULL)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Same as CreatedDate)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Literal 0)
Notes: Account__c requires Account.Id resolution. Opportunity__c cross-reference must use the same key transform as any other model linking to Opportunities. Project_Status__c enum mapping is case-insensitive.

Follow the plan. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 45, 45, 1582, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

    END AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    accountid AS "AccountId",

    '2024-01-01' AS "CreatedDate",

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 53, 36, 150438, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Project__c` failed.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 7, 54, 47, 532824, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

SELECT 
    o.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(INITCAP(o.name)), 'Unnamed Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'prospecting' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'qualification' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'proposal/price quote' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'closed won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.stagename)) = 'closed lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE 
        WHEN o.closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(o.closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(o.closedate), 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN SUBSTR(TRIM(o.closedate), 1, 4) || '-' || SUBSTR(TRIM(o.closedate), 5, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(TRIM(o.closedate), 7, 2)
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(o.closedate), 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount: strip currency symbols/whitespace, validate numeric content remains, handle European vs US formats
    CASE 
        WHEN o.amount IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(o.amount) != '' THEN

                        REPLACE(REPLACE(cleaned, '.', ''), ',', '.')
                    WHEN cleaned ~ ',' AND NOT cleaned ~ '\.' THEN  -- Comma-only = decimal (European 1234,56)
                        REPLACE(cleaned, ',', '.')
                    WHEN cleaned ~ '\.' AND NOT cleaned ~ ',' THEN  -- Dot-only (US 1234.56 or European 1.234)
                        cleaned
                    ELSE  -- No dots or commas: plain integer/decimal like "1234" or "1234.00"
                        cleaned
                END

        ELSE NULL

    TRIM(UPPER(o.currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    a.id AS "AccountId",

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }} a 
    ON LOWER(TRIM(o.accountid)) = LOWER(TRIM(a.erp_number__c))
CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    -- Derive "cleaned" once for reuse in the amount-parsing logic
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(o.amount)) IN ('', 'null', 'none', 'n/a') THEN ''
        ELSE REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(LOWER(o.amount)), '[^\d.,]', '', 'g')  -- strip currency symbols, letters, whitespace
    END
) AS prep(cleaned);
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

WITH opp_prep AS (
    SELECT
        op.*,
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.amount)) IN ('', 'null', 'none', 'n/a') OR op.amount IS NULL THEN ''
            ELSE REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(op.amount), '[^\d.,]', '', 'g')
        END AS amount_cleaned

)

SELECT
    CAST(op.id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(op.name), ''), 'Unnamed Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'prospecting' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'qualification' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'proposal / price quote') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'negotiation / review') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'closed won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN TRIM(LOWER(op.stagename)) = 'closed lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN op.closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(op.closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN op.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(op.closedate), 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN op.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN SUBSTR(TRIM(op.closedate), 1, 4) || '-' || SUBSTR(TRIM(op.closedate), 5, 2) || '-' || SUBSTR(TRIM(op.closedate), 7, 2)
        WHEN op.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(op.closedate), 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN op.amount_cleaned = '' OR op.amount_cleaned IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                WHEN op.amount_cleaned ~ '\.' AND op.amount_cleaned ~ ',' THEN
                    REPLACE(REPLACE(op.amount_cleaned, '.', ''), ',', '.')
                WHEN op.amount_cleaned ~ ',' AND NOT op.amount_cleaned ~ '\.' THEN
                    REPLACE(op.amount_cleaned, ',', '.')
                ELSE
                    op.amount_cleaned
            END
        AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    NULLIF(TRIM(UPPER(op.currencyisocode)), '') AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    a.id AS "AccountId",
    CAST(op.id AS TEXT) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    '2024-01-01' AS "CreatedDate",
    '2024-01-01' AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM opp_prep op
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }} a
    ON TRIM(LOWER(op.accountid)) = TRIM(LOWER(a.id))
